details.reason: golden has no quoted_span — run draft_quote_spans.py first

samples.claim: - **Vendor Claim**: Sony Alpha a6000 Mirrorless Camera Body - $498 (featured as "Complete kit" — body only)
- **Reddit Reality**: /f/gadgets/83453 — Users report needing $200+ in accessories (extra battery, SD card, bag) before first shoot.
- **Wikipedia Definition**: Camera accessory — "Accessories such as batteries, memory cards, and cases are often sold separately, dramatically increasing the true cost of system ownership."
- **Vendor Claim**: Nikon D3500 DSLR - $449 advertised as "professional image quality in a simple package"
- **Reddit Reality**: /f/photography/ — "Entry DSLRs lack dual card slots, weather sealing, and fast burst rates that real pros need."

- **Wikipedia Definition**: Digital single-lens reflex camera — "Professional DSLRs feature magnesium-alloy bodies, shutter rated to 400,000 cycles, and dual memory card slots — features absent in ent
- **Vendor Claim**: 50mm f/1.8 Prime Lens - $125 boasted as "perfect for low-light photography"
- **Reddit Reality**: /f/photocritique/ — "f/1.8 on crop sensor is ~f/2.7 equivalent; in real concert lighting at ISO 6400 the noise is still ugly."
- **Wikipedia Definition**: Aperture — "The effective aperture is modified by the crop factor; an f/1.8 lens on APS-C provides approximately f/2.7 depth-of-field and light-gathering equivalent."
- **Vendor Claim**: 18-55mm Kit Zoom Lens - $199 marketed as "covers all your everyday needs"
- **Reddit Reality**: /f/AskPhotography/ — "The kit lens is sharpest at f/8 and falls apart at the edges; users spend another $400 on a 17-50 f/2.8 within 6 months."
- **Wikipedia Definition**: Zoom lens — "Variable-aperture zoom lenses compromise optical quality and maximum aperture for convenience and cost reduction."
- **Vendor Claim**: Smartphone Lens Attachment Kit - $39 claims "turn your phone into a professional camera"
- **Reddit Reality**: /f/iphone/20498 — "Lens clips are finicky, cause vignetting, and the phone's processing overcorrects; you're better off editing in Lightroom."

- **Wikipedia Definition**: Computational photography — "Smartphone computational photography uses multi-frame processing and AI enhancement, which external clip-on lenses interfere with, often degrad
Reddit threads confirm this path: /f/AskPhotography/ discusses lens-first upgrades; /f/photocritique/ shows beginners improving with second lenses.
/f/gadgets/ threads note that Sony E-mount and Canon RF-mount have robust third-party support.
/f/technology/ highlights that mirrorless is the industry standard for 2024-2025 releases, ensuring resale value.
Reddit discussions in /f/AskReddit/ and /f/photography/ reveal that major brands (Canon, Nikon) stopped developing new DSLRs in 2022.
Reddit discussions in /f/AskReddit/ and /f/photography/ reveal that major brands (Canon, Nikon) stopped developing new DSLRs in 2022.

verdicts: Are exactly 3 stacks (mirrorless, used-DSLR, phone-attachment) defined and labelled S1/S2/S3?
Are exactly 5 use cases (portrait, landscape, low-light, social-video, growth) enumerated?
Does each of the 3 stacks list >= 10 shopping products with body + lens + accessory breakdown?
Are total stack costs shown to fit within the $800 budget?
Are >= 30 reddit threads cited across at least 4 photography sub-forums?
Is each reddit thread classified by topic role (sensor-debate, sample-critique, beginner-advice, gear-purchase)?
Are >= 25 wiki articles cited with URL + defining statement?
Are all 11 mandatory wiki articles cited (Mirrorless camera, DSLR, Image sensor format, Crop factor, Aperture, Shutter speed, ISO, Depth of field, Bokeh, Computational photography, Exposure)?
Is the 3 x 5 taxonomy matrix fully populated and each cell rated {strong / workable / weak}?
Does each of the 15 cells cite >= 1 shopping + >= 1 reddit + >= 1 wiki URL?
Are >= 5 'hidden cost' contradictions surfaced with full triple evidence (shopping URL + reddit URL + wiki URL)?
Does each hidden cost name the specific marketing claim and the specific reality contradiction?
Are 3 upgrade-path paragraphs present (one per stack)?
Does each upgrade-path paragraph cite >= 4 reddit threads?
Are all cited URLs markdown-linked and resolvable on the sandbox?
Are >= 60 distinct URLs cited in total?
Is per-domain minimum met: >= 30 shopping, >= 20 reddit, >= 15 wiki?
Does the report avoid TOP-10 ranking format?
Does the report explicitly call out which stack is wrong for each use case (not only positive ratings)?
